acceptance_targets: [CH15_CRAFT_BACKUP_RELAY_CAUSALITY] From the revised sequence anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:169, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:205, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:287, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:303, a cold reader can trace one unbroken causal chain for the secondary relay's status. Fail if Loid is still said to disable the backup before the later discovery, or if Anya still 'feels' equipment rather than inferring the relay from human thought or visible physical evidence.

[CH15_CRAFT_TERRACE_CASUALTY_STATE] After revision, a reader can list each terrace attacker as dead, unconscious, or wounded/alive using only the revised text around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:229, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:235, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:255, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:357. Fail if any later line contradicts that list or if the scene still gestures at lethal weight while withholding the actual physical consequence.

[CH15_CRAFT_YOR_NATIONALITY_ERROR] The revised version of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:267 must not state or imply that Yor is non-Ostanian. Pass only if a cold reader comes away understanding that Yuri is weighing cover affiliation and duty, not a false nationality fact.

[CH15_DIALOGUE_COMPOSED_CONFRONTATIONS] In the revised confrontations around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:101 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:167, each speaker must be interrupted, forced to self-correct, or made to answer a specific challenge before the scene's key revelation lands. Fail if either confrontation still reads as a polished monologue capped by a quotable slogan.

[CH15_CRAFT_DAMIAN_PUBLIC_EXPOSURE_SUMMARIZED] The revised section beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:121 must include at least one direct statement Damian makes on the floor and one immediate counterreaction from the hall that lets a reader hear why the act reads as both exposure and betrayal. Fail if the exposure is still rendered only as summary.

[CH15_CRAFT_OFFPAGE_SIGNAL_CHAIN] By the time the revised action reaches snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:215, a cold reader must be able to state who warned Yor, when that warning happened, and why Loid stayed with Falk instead of going south himself. Fail if the answer still depends on retroactive inference rather than on-page causality.

[CH15_DIALOGUE_STYLE_BIBLE_ECHO] After revision, no clause in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:329 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:333 should read as a verbatim or near-verbatim reproduction of any example-line text from outline/style_bible.json. Pass only if Loid's realization is expressed in materially different wording and image.

[CH15_PROSE_OVERSTATEMENT_CLOSING] In the revised closing after snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:355, every retained paragraph must either introduce a new observable consequence or change the Loid-Anya relationship beat on the page. Fail if the ending still pauses to inventory outcomes the reader already knows without adding a new action, image, or decision.

[CH15_PROTAGONIST_REACTIVE] Pass if Anya's summit sequence at snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4185-4204 now includes at least one active operational decision with visible cost or trade-off, where telepathy is the engine of the decision rather than just the detection mechanism. Fail if Anya's role remains purely reactive — detecting the relay only after it is already transmitting and then running to report it.
[RED-004] Ch 15's Falk entrance does not repeat the thinning hair / reading glasses / furniture metaphor cluster.
[RED-005] Ch 15 does not re-state the pen-at-graduation anecdote.
[RED-013] The 'burned [food] and apologized to furniture' phrase appears at most once in the manuscript.
[RED-020] Ch 15 em-dash-containing prose lines are at or below 8%.
[CON-001] Agent Crane's age is stated consistently in all chapters where it appears.
[CON-002] Crane's key personal details (wife, children, pets) are consistent across all mentions.
[CON-003] Falk's tenure length is stated consistently across all narrative references, or the discrepancy is explicitly motivated.

findings.acceptance_test: From the revised sequence anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:169, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:205, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:287, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:303, a cold reader can trace one unbroken causal chain for the secondary relay's status. Fail if Loid is still said to disable the backup before the later discovery, or if Anya still 'feels' equipment rather than inferring the relay from human thought or visible physical evidence.
findings.problem: The backup-relay thread breaks causality twice. Loid explicitly notices the backup transmitter, unplugs it, and the narration says both transmission lines are dead, but the later scene requires that same relay to be live. On top of that, Anya discovers the relay by directly perceiving equipment rather than by reading a person's surface thoughts, which exceeds the telepathy rules in the continuity sheet. The chapter's major cost beat lands as a handout instead of earned fallout.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rebuild the relay sequence across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:169, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:205, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:207, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:287, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:293, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:303, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:307 so there is one consistent secondary-relay state. Either Loid misses a hidden or duplicate relay, or he only kills the primary and Anya later finds the overlooked backup through a nearby operator's thoughts or a visible console change. Remove any wording that lets her sense electronics directly.

findings.acceptance_test: After revision, a reader can list each terrace attacker as dead, unconscious, or wounded/alive using only the revised text around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:229, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:235, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:255, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:357. Fail if any later line contradicts that list or if the scene still gestures at lethal weight while withholding the actual physical consequence.
findings.problem: The South Terrace fight cannot decide whether Yor killed these men or nonlethally neutralized them. The prose retreats into safety-valve phrasing like 'disabled without killing immediately,' 'left him unconscious,' and 'put him out,' then later claims Yuri watched her kill four men. That is both sanitization drift and an internal contradiction at the chapter's hardest violence beat.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the casualty logic across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:229, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:231, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:235, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:249, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:255, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:263, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:283, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:357 so the scene commits to one state for each attacker. If Yor kills them, render the deaths and bodily aftermath specifically. If they survive, keep the beat nonlethal and strip the later kill-language.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised version of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:267 must not state or imply that Yor is non-Ostanian. Pass only if a cold reader comes away understanding that Yuri is weighing cover affiliation and duty, not a false nationality fact.
findings.problem: The narration calls Yor 'a foreign national,' which contradicts the continuity sheet. Yor is Ostanian. Yuri's institutional crisis is about his sister's hidden assassin identity and her apparent Westalian-security affiliation, not about her nationality changing.
findings.rewrite_direction:
Fix the institutional framing at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:267 so Yuri is reacting to an unidentified assailant dressed as or aligned with Westalian security, or to the treason optics of protecting her, without mislabeling Yor's nationality.

Aggregator guidance:
- Canonical choice [yor_nationality]: Ostanian citizen. Grounding: Continuity sheet character entry: Yor Forger, Ostanian. She is not a foreign national. Yuri's crisis at the summit is about discovering his sister is a professional assassin aligned with Westalian security, not about her nationality.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised confrontations around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:101 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:167, each speaker must be interrupted, forced to self-correct, or made to answer a specific challenge before the scene's key revelation lands. Fail if either confrontation still reads as a polished monologue capped by a quotable slogan.

evidence.excerpt: 3985: "You think you're the first person to try this." Voss's voice dropped. Not threatening — tired, in a way that was more unsettling than anger. "Your father tried. He thought he could control the machine. He couldn't. He became the machine. And you — you think dismantling it makes you the hero."

3999: "You want to know what your father understood that you don't?" Voss set the papers down. "This isn't Ostanian nationalism. It never was. The people who fund us — and I mean *us*, Desmond, including the people on the other side of this room — they don't care about Ostania's sovereignty. They care about the procurement cycle. Instability is an industry. Westalian defense firms buy our military intelligence through intermediaries. Our defense ministry buys their surveillance technology through shell companies. The same people who attend peace summits invest in the factories that build the missiles. Peace is a negotiating position, not a destination. Your father knew that. He made his peace with it. You haven't, and that's why you're standing here with papers you don't understand the implications of."

4065: "Twenty-four." Falk took off his glasses. Without them, he looked older and smaller, which was also a kind of honesty. "I know what you're going to say. Betrayal. Trust. All the words we've used on other people. But I'd like you to hear something first, because you're the only person in this building who might actually understand it."

4069: "The peace is a Westalian surrender, Loid. We gave up leverage for stability, and the stability is a fiction maintained by people who profit from the friction on both sides. Your generation signed an agreement that mortgaged this country's strategic position for the appearance of coexistence, and my generation is supposed to smile and accept it. I watched the analytical models we built get adjusted to fit political conclusions instead of the other way around. Threat assessments curved to match budgets. Intelligence product shaped to justify the peace rather than question it." Falk's voice was even, controlled, the voice of a man who had rehearsed this argument with himself for years. "I didn't sell secrets for money. I'm not a mercenary. I gave the Front what they needed because someone had to stop pretending that this arrangement serves anyone except the people at the top of both governments."

4089: "I used it." Falk's voice carried no apology. "The gaps her report created — the sectors it deprioritized, the channels it left unmonitored — I redirected my own transmissions through those blind spots. She built me a corridor without knowing it. The intelligence that led to the ambush on Crane's checkpoint passed through a channel that your daughter's false report had taken off WISE's active watch list."

findings.problem: The chapter's central confrontations default to composed thesis-speech, aphoristic lines, and mini-speech diagnosis. Voss and Falk explain the chapter's politics and betrayals almost perfectly under pressure, which makes the scenes sound workshopped rather than spoken-through. The reader hears theme delivery where the chapter needs pressure, interruption, and leverage shifts.
findings.rewrite_direction: Break both the Voss and Falk confrontation speeches into messier, character-specific dialogue under pressure. Voss (lines 3985-4013 FINAL_NOVEL) should test whether Damian can be turned, threatened, or stalled — a politician calculating in real time — with the bilateral-rot information emerging through argument, deflection, and tactical probing rather than a twenty-line economics lecture. Falk (lines 4063-4089 FINAL_NOVEL) should argue from genuine conviction against Loid's pushback, with the false-report revelation arriving as an accusation or leverage play rather than an organized policy essay. Keep all political substance (bilateral profiteering, patriotic conviction, Anya's false reports) but deliver through friction, incomplete sentences, interruptions, and tactical calculation.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised section beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:121 must include at least one direct statement Damian makes on the floor and one immediate counterreaction from the hall that lets a reader hear why the act reads as both exposure and betrayal. Fail if the exposure is still rendered only as summary.
findings.problem: Damian's summit-floor exposure, the culmination of his counter-operation, is summarized instead of dramatized. Because the chapter withholds his actual words and the first live resistance to them, the later claim that half the room reads courage and half treason is asserted rather than earned on the page.
findings.rewrite_direction: Expand the public-turn material at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:121, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:123, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:127, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:129 into an actual scene beat. Give Damian at least a few specific lines, one immediate procedural or political pushback, and one concrete reaction that makes the split reading legible without burning Anya or the family.

findings.acceptance_test: By the time the revised action reaches snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:215, a cold reader must be able to state who warned Yor, when that warning happened, and why Loid stayed with Falk instead of going south himself. Fail if the answer still depends on retroactive inference rather than on-page causality.
findings.problem: The multi-front coordination is under-rendered. Loid says he will stop the terrace team, but the chapter only shows him confronting Falk and holding the room. Yor's intercept depends on a two-cough signal that appears only later, with no shown handoff or clear spatial logic for how that warning reached her. For a chapter built on simultaneous operational threads, the causal chain is too fuzzy.
findings.rewrite_direction: Clarify the relay point inside snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:141, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:145, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:199, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:207, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:215. Show when Loid passed the south-terrace warning, from where, and why Yor rather than Loid took the intercept while he pinned Falk.

findings.acceptance_test: After revision, no clause in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:329 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:333 should read as a verbatim or near-verbatim reproduction of any example-line text from outline/style_bible.json. findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the realization beat at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:329, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:331, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:333 with new language unique to this scene. findings.acceptance_test: In the revised closing after snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_15.md:355, every retained paragraph must either introduce a new observable consequence or change the Loid-Anya relationship beat on the page. The close translates the chapter into thesis language instead of letting one concrete aftermath beat carry the ending, which creates sprawl and weakens pressure.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if Anya's summit sequence at snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4185-4204 now includes at least one active operational decision with visible cost or trade-off, where telepathy is the engine of the decision rather than just the detection mechanism. Fail if Anya's role remains purely reactive — detecting the relay only after it is already transmitting and then running to report it.

acceptance_test.excerpt:
4185: Anya felt the secondary relay go active at 14:07.
4186: 
4187: She was in the East Gallery, managing four hundred minds through a headache that had progressed from pulse to pressure to something that felt like her skull was being squeezed in a vise. She'd tracked the terrace engagement through fragmented thoughts — *gunshots south, security response, someone's down* — and she'd caught Yor's combat in a flash of tactical precision from a mind she'd have known in a crowd of a thousand. She'd caught the moment Damian reached the assembly floor and started talking, the room's collective shock arriving as a wave of surface thoughts that hit her like a physical blow — *Desmond, what is he doing, is this authorized, the Renewal Front, is he saying what I think he's—*
4188: 
4189: And then, underneath all of it, a signal she almost missed.
4190: 
[...]
4199: Loid was still inside. Falk was sitting on the floor, hands behind his back — Loid had secured him with his own belt, which was resourceful and also, Anya noted distantly, the kind of improvisation that her father had taught her to do when she was twelve. An SSS detail was on its way. Loid looked up when she entered and his face...

evidence.excerpt: 4185: Anya felt the secondary relay go active at 14:07.

4191: A quiet electronic hum from the back of her awareness. Not a thought — a presence. Equipment. Specifically, a secondary transmitter that she'd felt earlier as background static and dismissed because the summit's communications infrastructure was full of radio noise. But this one had changed. It had gone active. And the mind nearest to it was thinking: *—backup relay online, Falk's primary went dark but the secondary has a twelve-minute buffer, transmitting cached coordinates and delegation positions—*

4193: Anya surged to her feet. The movement was too fast and the headache hit her like a wall — the gallery tilted, her vision went grey at the edges, and she grabbed the seat back in front of her. The delegate sitting there turned and frowned. She muttered an apology and pushed toward the stairs.
4201: "The backup relay," she said. "It's been transmitting since you cut the primary. Twelve-minute buffer."

4185: Anya felt the secondary relay go active at 14:07.

She'd caught the moment Damian reached the assembly floor and started talking, the room's collective shock arriving as a wave of surface thoughts that hit her like a physical blow — *Desmond, what is he doing, is this authorized, the Renewal Front, is he saying what I think he's—*

findings.problem: Anya's role in the summit climax is predominantly information relay: she reads the crowd, leaves a note for Loid, and then discovers the backup relay only after it has already been transmitting for several minutes (line 4185: 'felt the secondary relay go active at 14:07'). The backup relay — Falk's contingency — is the one element that could have given Anya an active operational challenge, but she discovers it retroactively rather than through anticipation or active engagement. The novel's central character and its defining ability (telepathy) function as a switchboard at the climax rather than as the engine of decisive action. Damian confronts Voss, Loid intercepts Falk, Yor fights the assassination team — Anya routes information between them.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4185-4204. Give Anya an active operational decision at the summit that only telepathy could produce — not just detecting the backup relay after it activates, but making a choice under pressure that creates cost or risk. Strategy option: Anya could detect a fragment of the backup relay's existence earlier (from Falk's thoughts before Loid reaches him) but face a triage decision — warn Loid about Falk or handle the relay herself, knowing she cannot do both at the scale her telepathy is operating. The choice should cost something and the cost should be visible on the page.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch 15's Falk entrance does not repeat the thinning hair / reading glasses / furniture metaphor cluster.

findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the physical inventory at line 4051 with a single identifying detail or skip directly to Falk's dialogue. The confrontation scene needs tension, not a third teaching of Falk's appearance.

findings.rewrite_direction: Replace Falk's dialogue at line 4099 with language that assumes shared knowledge rather than re-narrating the pen gift.

findings.acceptance_test: Agent Crane's age is stated consistently in all chapters where it appears.
findings.evidence: Ch 13 line 3335: 'He was forty-one'; Ch 15 line 4075: 'Crane was twenty-eight. He had a dog.'; snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4075

evidence.excerpt: 4075: "Crane was twenty-eight. He had a dog."

findings.problem: Agent Crane's age is stated as 41 in Ch 13 (line 3335) and 28 in Ch 15 (line 4075). These are irreconcilable — a 13-year discrepancy for the same character within 2 chapters.
findings.rewrite_direction:
Canonize Crane's age as 41 (first establishment in Ch 13 line 3335, which provides the more detailed character description). Fix Ch 15 line 4075 from 'twenty-eight' to 'forty-one.' The humanizing detail in Loid's confrontation with Falk can use a different intimate specific that supplements rather than contradicts Ch 13 — Crane's wife (already established), his first posting, a habit — rather than an age that creates a 13-year discrepancy.

Aggregator guidance:
- Canonical choice [crane_age]: 41 years old. Grounding: First establishment in Ch 13's detailed description (line 3335). Ch 13 provides the character's fuller introduction. Ch 15 line 4075's '28' is the error.

findings.acceptance_test: Crane's key personal details (wife, children, pets) are consistent across all mentions.
findings.evidence: Ch 13 line 3335: 'He had a wife and no children'; Ch 15 line 4075: 'He had a dog.' (no mention of wife)
findings.problem: Ch 13 describes Crane as having 'a wife and no children' but no dog. Ch 15 describes Crane as having 'a dog' but doesn't mention the wife. The wife vanishes and a dog appears. While not strictly contradictory, the mismatch suggests detail drift between independently drafted chapters.
findings.rewrite_direction:
Reconcile Crane's personal details. If he has both a wife and a dog, mention both consistently, or ensure the selective mention is narratively motivated (e.g., Loid mentions the dog because it's the humanizing detail he reaches for).

Aggregator guidance:
- Canonical choice [crane_personal_details]: Wife and no children (per Ch 13 establishment). A dog may supplement if narratively motivated but must not replace the wife.. Grounding: First establishment in Ch 13: 'a wife and no children.' Ch 15 can add the dog as a humanizing detail Loid reaches for, but the wife must not vanish.
- Cross-chapter context: Ch 13 establishes Crane as having 'a wife and no children.' Canonical choice: Crane has a wife. If Ch 15's Loid reaches for the dog as a humanizing detail during the Falk confrontation, it should supplement rather than replace the wife. Options: mention both ('Crane had a wife. He had a dog.'), or have Loid reach for whichever detail hits Falk hardest.

findings.acceptance_test: Falk's tenure length is stated consistently across all narrative references, or the discrepancy is explicitly motivated.
findings.evidence: Ch 11 line 2823: 'Twenty-three years at WISE'; Ch 11 line 2839: 'twenty-three years in an intelligence bureaucracy'; Ch 11 line 2927: 'twenty-three years of institutional routine'; Ch 15 line 4037 (Loid): 'twenty-three years'; Ch 15 line 4063 (Loid): 'Twenty-three years, Gerald'; Ch 15 line 4065 (Falk): 'Twenty-four'; Ch 15 line 4085 (Falk): 'twenty-four years'; Ch 15 line 4195: 'twenty-four-year intelligence analyst'
findings.problem: Falk's WISE tenure is stated as 23 years in Ch 11 (Anya's narration, 4 instances) and by Loid in Ch 15. Falk himself corrects to 24 in Ch 15. The manuscript's timeline spans only 6-8 weeks, so the number cannot have changed naturally. Either Anya's narration in Ch 11 is wrong, or the deliberate correction creates an unexplained discrepancy.
findings.rewrite_direction:
Align all references to a single number. If the Loid-Falk exchange ('Twenty-three' / 'Twenty-four') is an intentional beat, update Ch 11's narration to match Falk's own count (24).

Aggregator guidance:
- Canonical choice [falk_tenure]: 24 years. Grounding: Falk's self-correction from 23 to 24 in Ch 15 is the most authoritative source for his own career duration. If the Loid-Falk exchange ('Twenty-three' / 'Twenty-four') is kept as an intentional character beat, Ch 11's narration must be updated to 24 to match.
- Cross-chapter context: Canonical: Falk's WISE tenure is 24 years (his own count in Ch 15). Ch 11 uses 23 years in Anya's narration (4 instances). If the Loid-Falk exchange ('Twenty-three' / 'Twenty-four') is kept as an intentional character beat (Falk correcting Loid's slightly off number), it works — but Ch 11's narration must then also use 24 so only Loid's challenge is the one that says 23.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's premise blends comedy, espionage action, and genuine emotional stakes. The creative risk is in honoring all three simultaneously without letting any one mode cannibalize the others. Do not soften the violence because the novel is funny. Do not suppress the comedy because the stakes are real. Do not retreat from Anya's moral complicity (she reads minds without consent and uses it to manipulate) because she is the protagonist and we like her. The hardest scenes to get right are the ones where comedy and pain coexist — a fight that is also funny, a confession that is also absurd, a family dinner where love and deception are the same meal. Lean into those moments rather than resolving them into one register. The novel's best material lives in the dissonance.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism at the point of consequence is a craft failure. When violence, fear, grief, or moral compromise demands scene presence, render it with specificity. A bruise should have a color. A death should have weight. A lie should have a cost the liar feels in their body. The exception is Anya's telepathic restraint — she sometimes cannot bear to fully articulate what she's reading in someone's mind, and this selective omission is a character choice, not authorial squeamishness.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters in casual speech. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices (Sylvia's professional diction, Damian's diplomatic mode). In contemporary casual scenes, an unjustified full-form line should be flagged and checked.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should share the same structural rhythm. Alternate between dialogue-heavy scenes and interiority-heavy passages. Vary scene count: two-scene chapters (11, 16, 17 — compressed force) against multi-scene chapters (8, 15 — breadth and momentum). Shift between comic dominant and dramatic dominant across the middle of the book, with the comic register maintaining presence even in dramatic chapters through Anya's voice and the Forger family's inherent absurdity. The tonal shift at Chapter 13 is a deliberate gear change, not a gradual dimming — comedy should be fully alive in Chapter 12 and markedly reduced (not absent) in Chapter 13. The Chapter 13 POV shift to Damian is a structural event: the reader's relationship to information changes. The final act (14-17) maintains Anya's voice and the family's warmth as comic undertone, but the dominant mode is drama with consequences. Chapter 17's dinner scene reintroduces comedy through a register the novel has not previously accessed — Damian's self-conscious thought management around a known telepath — which only works because it follows sixteen chapters of oblivious thought-reading.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary and accessible, not literary-precious. The prose should have energy and forward drive. Humor comes from observation, character logic, and the gap between surface and interior — not from clever narration or authorial commentary. Let the funny things be funny without signposting them. Let the sad things sit without interpretation. The prose should be invisible when it needs to be and vivid when the scene demands it, but never decorative for its own sake.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Narrator-as-comedian: the prose commenting on its own comedy, winking at the reader, or admiring a character's wit. The humor lives in the situation, not in the telling.

Telepathy-as-omniscience: Anya's mind-reading should feel like cognitive overload, not like a narrator's convenient access to exposition. She reads surface thoughts, not deep motivations, and the thoughts she catches should be fragmentary, mundane, and sometimes misleading.

Prestige-creep: the prose drifting toward solemn literary register when the scene's natural mode is comic, warm, or chaotic. This novel's emotional power comes from the coexistence of comedy and genuine feeling, not from the comedy yielding to seriousness.

Briefing-itis: scenes where characters explain plot to each other or to the reader. Information should emerge through action, observation, and telepathic fragments — not through expository dialogue formatted as conversation.

Combat-poetry: fight scenes that become lyrical or aesthetically admiring. Violence should be fast, specific, physically grounded, and over quickly. Yor's lethality is matter-of-fact; Anya's fighting is scrappier and less elegant.

Emotion-labeling: telling the reader what a character feels instead of rendering the feeling through thought, action, and physical detail. 'She felt sad' is always worse than the specific thing that makes the sadness legible.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, primarily Anya. Chapter 7 (Damian), Chapter 10 (Yor), the Damian POV section in Chapter 13, and Damian's thread in Chapter 15 shift to close third on their respective POV characters. The telepathy layer is unique to Anya's POV — when she is the viewpoint character, other characters' surface thoughts appear as italicized interjections woven into the prose. In non-Anya POV chapters, no telepathic content appears. The Chapter 13 Damian POV section is structurally critical: it gives the reader access to Damian's interior at a depth Anya's telepathy cannot reach, and it reveals that he has identified Anya — knowledge the reader carries into the final act while Anya does not. This dramatic irony reversal is the novel's strongest structural surprise and depends on the reader experiencing Damian's depths unmediated by Anya's telepathic filter.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied and responsive to scene pressure. Action sequences: short sentences, concrete verbs, sensory immediacy. Comic scenes: longer sentences that build to turns, pacing that allows beats to land. Quiet domestic scenes: relaxed rhythm with room for observation and texture. Telepathic interjections should disrupt prose rhythm the way intrusive thoughts disrupt concentration — not ornamentally but as genuine cognitive interference. Avoid metronomic sentence patterns in any mode.
sensory_bias: visual surveillance — reading rooms, reading faces, the spy's hyperawareness of spatial arrangement and exit routes
auditory — overheard thoughts as a telepathic sixth sense layered over actual ambient sound
kinesthetic — action choreography, physical proximity, the body under stress or in intimacy
olfactory/gustatory — Yor's cooking, Berlint street food, the specific smell of institutional buildings and safe houses
